metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_5178
metadata.comment:
Rationale: Figure 3: Slc4a8 is important for the regulation of pHi in hippocampal neurons.

Figure 4, 5: Altered excitability of CA1 pyramidal cells in the absence of Slc4a8.

Figure 6: Slc4a8 modulates presynaptic vesicle release.

"To finally pinpoint the defect of glutamatergic transmission in KO mice to the presynapse, vesicle release upon field stimulation was subsequently quantified by measuring bouton destaining in FM1-43-labeled cultured hippocampal neurons (Fig. 6A,B). No difference was observed in baseline punctual intensity (...). However, destaining, and hence synaptic vesicle release, upon repetitive stimulation was significantly diminished in KO cultures after ~900 action potentials (...). Following extensive stimulation with >1500 action potentials, this difference disappeared (...).
...
These findings could be corroborated electrophysiologically: normalized evoked EPSC amplitudes decreased more rapidly per stimulus upon application of the open-channel NMDA receptor blocker MK-801 in WT compared with KO slices (Fig. 6C). The blocking rate of NMDA receptor-mediated EPSCs was significantly delayed in KO slices (...) compared with WT (...), whereas the input/output relationship of evoked NMDA current density did not differ between genotypes at all stimulus intensities tested (...)."

authors conclude:
"Together, these experiments show that deletion of Slc4a8 results in a reduction of the number of released glutamatergic vesicles, although the pool of releasable vesicles appears to be unchanged (Fig. 6D).". Experimental description: M&M: "For immunohistochemistry, brains of 6- to 8-week-old wild-type (WT) and KO mice were ..."

antibody:
polyclonal rabbit Slc4a8 antibody (Leviel et al., 2010). PMID:20389022
Figure 1: validation of the Slc4a8 antibody using westernblot of wildtype and knockout mouse brains.
